claims.claim: For NCM811|AG battery with 1M LiPF6 EC/EMC electrolyte, the three characteristic temperatures were T1=65°C (onset of self-heating), T2=218°C (thermal runaway trigger, defined as when temperature rise rate reached 1°C/s), and T3=750°C (maximum temperature).
claims.quote: In this study, the three characteristic temperatures were located at 65 ℃ (T1), 218 ℃ (T2) and 750 ℃ (T3), respectively.

claims.claim: The reaction between cathode and electrolyte was identified as the thermal runaway trigger for NCM811|AG battery, based on DSC showing a sharp exothermic peak at 215°C for the Ca+Ely and Ca+Ely+An samples, which appeared earlier than the cathode-alone peak at ~230°C.
claims.quote: for the Ca +Ely sample, two peaks appeared at 215 ℃ and 230 ℃. And for the Ca +Ely +An sample, a sharp exothermic peak was also detected at 215 ℃. Based on the time order of the reactions, the reaction between cathode and electrolyte was identified as the thermal runaway trigger.

claims.claim: When TPFPB was added to the electrolyte, the oxygen gas release peak from charged NCM811 cathode shifted to a lower temperature of ~150°C compared to ~230°C for cathode with electrolyte alone, because TPFPB acted as a reducing agent that reduced highly reactive oxygen species into oxygen gas at lower temperature.
claims.quote: Big changes were observed when the electrolyte mixed with TPFPB. As shown in Fig. 3e, the oxygen gas peak starts earlier at ~150 ◦C. This behavior is caused by the TPFPB serving as a reduction agent that reduces the highly reactive oxygen species into oxygen gas.

claims.claim: Adding TPFPB to the NCM811 battery electrolyte lowered T2 to 198°C (20°C lower than the 218°C for standard NCM811), indicating that TPFPB addition made the battery more susceptible to thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: The key temperature, T2, was 198 ◦C for B-NCM811, 20 ◦C lower than for NCM811, indicating that the battery was more likely to thermal runaway after TPFPB added.

claims.claim: In the presence of electrolyte, the lattice strain in the NCM811 cathode was released spontaneously by cation migration, phase transformation, and oxygen release, whereas the dry cathode could endure a much greater lattice strain, indicating that electrolyte accelerates phase transition and oxygen release.
claims.quote: the dry cathode lattice can endure a much greater lattice strain, while the strain in the presence of electrolyte is released spontaneously by cation migration, phase transformation and oxygen release. Therefore, the results show that electrolyte has a negative impact on the phase structure stability of cathode, which could accelerate phase transition and oxygen release.

claims.claim: The thermal stability order of cathode materials is LFP > LMO > NCM111 > NCA > LCO, with LFP being the most stable cathode material during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The order of the thermal stability should be LFP>LMO>NCM111>NCA>LCO [63], with the LFP as the most stable cathode material during TR process.

claims.claim: For NCM cathode materials, the enthalpy of decomposition increases with increasing Ni content: Li0.37[Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3]O2 releases 512.5 J/g, while Li0.21[Ni0.85Co0.075Mn0.075]O2 releases 971.5 J/g.
claims.quote: The cathode with larger proportion of Ni in the NCM releases more heat during decomposition.

claims.claim: The initial decomposition of SEI occurs at 80–120°C with an exothermic peak at approximately 100°C, and may begin as low as 57°C though heat generation only becomes detectable at 80°C or higher.
claims.quote: The initial decomposition of SEI occurs at 80~120oC [79], with a peak locates at approximately 100oC [80]. The onset temperature can be lower than 80oC, as Wang et al. [76] reported that the SEI decomposition might start from a temperature as low as 57oC. However, the exothermic heat generation can only become detectable at 80oC or higher in the calorimetry.

claims.claim: The PE separator melts at approximately 130°C and the PP separator melts at approximately 170°C; both melting processes are endothermic with enthalpies of -90 J/g and -190 J/g respectively.
claims.quote: The melting points of the PE and PP separators are approximately 130oC and 170oC [95,127,128,129], respectively. The separator melting is an endothermic process... The enthalpy of the PE and PP separator during melting are -90 J∙g-1 and -190 J∙g-1, respectively

claims.claim: The collapse temperature of a separator with ceramic coating can be as high as 200–260°C, compared to approximately 130°C for PE and 170°C for PP/PE/PP separators without ceramic coating.
claims.quote: The collapse temperature of the separator with ceramic coating can be as high as 200-260oC [70, 129]. The ISC can occur at approximately 130oC (for the PE separator), 170oC (for the PP or PP/PE/PP separator), or as high as 200oC (for the separator with ceramic coating).

claims.claim: In NCM811/SiC cells, the internal temperature during thermal runaway can reach 1192°C, approximately 400°C higher than traditional NCM111-Graphite chemistries, with a TR onset temperature about 25°C lower.
claims.quote: during the TR of large format NCM811-SiC cells, the internal temperature could reach 1192 °C, almost 400 °C larger than the traditional NCM111-Graphite chemistries, with the TR onset temperature Tonset about 25 °C lower

claims.claim: Replacing SiC-electrolyte kinetics with graphite-electrolyte kinetics reduces total heat generation by approximately 29% (318 J) and decreases Tmax by 318°C, representing the largest single thermal contribution among the substitutions tested.
claims.quote: The 'Graphite-Ele' kinetic replacement shows the most obvious influence, and reduces the total heat by about 29% (318 J). While 'NCM111-Graphite', 'NCM111-Ele' kinetic substitutions result in heat decrease by 17% and 20%, respectively.

claims.claim: Replacing anode-electrolyte kinetics with graphite-electrolyte kinetics raises the TR onset temperature Tonset by 11.7% (from 179°C to 200°C) and reduces Tmax by 27.1%, while cathode-anode and cathode-electrolyte substitutions show no change in Tonset.

Replacing cathode-anode (NCM811-SiC) kinetics with NCM111-graphite kinetics reduces (dT/dt)max by 93.6%, indicating that cathode-anode interactions are the dominant driver of maximum temperature rising rate during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: (dT/dt)max is mainly influenced by the 'Ca-An' interactions ... Ca-An ... Difference ... −93.6%

claims.claim: The maximum internal temperature during thermal runaway of the 74 Ah NCM811/SiC cell was 490°C higher than the maximum surface temperature (1192°C internal vs. 702°C surface), and the internal (dT/dt)max was almost 20 times the surface (dT/dt)max.
claims.quote: The max temperature Tmax inside is 490 °C larger than the Tmax outside, while the internal temperature rising rate (dT/dt)max is almost 20 times the value of the (dT/dt)max outside.
